narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, including your LSAT score of 29 and coming from a lower-middle income family. The admissions model we use predicted a 70.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools rely on this to select students most likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from thousands of past students. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, both at the 1st decile—far below the typical 5th or 6th decile for admitted students—which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar struggles compared to peers with stronger early rankings. Your undergraduate GPA, while close to the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing the model saw some resilience there. In contrast, typical successful applicants often have higher decile rankings in those early years, pairing them with LSAT scores around 37 to balance out any gaps. Your full-time status aligns with most students who pass, so it didn't hurt, but it couldn't offset the weight of those low deciles. Overall, your profile stood out as riskier than the average because those early academic placements dominated the model's thinking, even with your decent GPA providing a counterbalance. This doesn't define you—many students in similar situations, including from underrepresented backgrounds like yours, have turned things around with targeted preparation. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for 35 or higher, as that could dramatically shift future predictions, and consider programs that bolster early academic records or offer bar prep support. Building a stronger narrative around your unique journey, perhaps through additional coursework or recommendations highlighting your growth, will also help. You're capable of beating these odds; use this feedback as a roadmap to strengthen your next application. Keep pushing—law schools value determined applicants like you who address challenges head-on.
